title: Will Our Hope on the Sri Lankan Question Come True?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. The Tamil reading copy below restores the source-printed opening salutation, restores the scan-confirmed date 12-3-2013, removes an OCR-only vertical marker, and corrects clear OCR character and word-boundary errors. The canonical source document remains unchanged. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Sri Lankan Foreign Minister G. L. Peiris met India's External Affairs Minister Sushma Swaraj on 11 July 2014. Although they spoke at length about the problems faced by Tamil Nadu's fishermen, the comments made afterwards by the External Affairs Ministry spokesperson have caused us sorrow.

Sri Lankan minister and Jathika Hela Urumaya general secretary Champika Ranawaka told reporters yesterday: "The India-Sri Lanka Accord was forcibly imposed on Sri Lanka. It was not an agreement willingly accepted by the people of the country. Nor does Wigneswaran have any right to demand powers for the Northern Provincial Council."

During Peiris's meeting with the Indian minister, they reportedly discussed at length a permanent solution to the Tamil fishermen's problem and the question of Sri Lankan Tamils. They also discussed amending the Thirteenth Amendment to Sri Lanka's Constitution. External Affairs spokesperson Syed Akbaruddin further told reporters that the Sri Lankan government proposed to establish a new township near Visakhapatnam in Andhra Pradesh, creating employment for 38,500 people.
Akbaruddin also said that Sushma Swaraj thanked Peiris for the release of a large number of Tamil Nadu fishermen held in Sri Lankan prisons after the BJP government assumed office at the Centre.

When reporters asked whether India would support the UN Human Rights Council's war-crimes investigation against Sri Lanka, some newspapers reported our External Affairs Minister as saying that, when a resolution seeking an investigation into war-crimes allegations came to a vote before the Council in March, India abstained and specifically voted against a clause providing for an expert team to be sent to Sri Lanka; and that India would not retreat from that policy.
If our External Affairs Minister did indeed say this, it confirms that the present government has adopted the same position as the previous United Progressive Alliance government.

In 2013 itself, when the United States was preparing a resolution in the UN Human Rights Council condemning the Sri Lankan government, our TESO movement demanded that India itself lead such a resolution. For that purpose, on 5 March 2013 we conducted a siege protest before the Sri Lankan Deputy High Commission and, on the same day, a protest before Parliament. Later, when more than thirty countries supported the American resolution, we urged India to support it too.

In Parliament, representatives of the DMK, AIADMK, Congress, Viduthalai Chiruthaigal Katchi, Rashtriya Janata Dal, Samajwadi Party, CPI(M), CPI, Trinamool Congress, Bahujan Samaj Party and even the BJP made forceful arguments condemning the Sri Lankan government. Speaking for the BJP, senior leader Yashwant Sinha said: "India must ask the Sri Lankan government immediately to withdraw the military stationed in the Northern Province. India must call for an impartial international investigation by persons from outside Sri Lanka into the genocide there. At the UN Human Rights Council, India must not merely be a country supporting a resolution against Sri Lanka; India itself must lead that resolution."

Even after this, the UPA government did not yield. Therefore, on 12 March 2013, TESO conducted a general strike. In Chennai, Kazhagam Treasurer Thalapathi Stalin, Tamil leader K. Veeramani, Thol. Thirumavalavan and Professor Suba. Veerapandian took part in a road blockade before Anbagam and were arrested.

In a statement on 16 March 2013, I urged the Indian government to secure an amendment to the proposed American resolution. It should refer to the recent genocide in Sri Lanka, identify the war criminals responsible, provide for an independent international investigation, and require appropriate action within a defined period.

Despite all these efforts by the DMK, whenever Tamil Nadu's ruling party and some others wished to criticise the Union government, they kept calling it "the Union government in which the DMK is a constituent," as though the DMK held more than a hundred seats and determined the government's survival. They habitually slandered us by saying that the DMK could have stopped all the killings in Sri Lanka if it had wished.

Disregarding such attacks, I issued a statement on 15 March 2013 insisting that India support the American resolution with suitable amendments. I said that if there were no favourable response from the Union government, it would be meaningless for the DMK to remain in the Union Cabinet. Union ministers Ghulam Nabi Azad, A. K. Antony and P. Chidambaram immediately met me in person and discussed the Eelam Tamil issue.

I told them: "India must declare the attacks carried out against Sri Lankan Tamils by the Sri Lankan government and those within its administration to be war crimes and genocide. India must amend the American resolution to call for a credible and independent international commission to investigate them, and Parliament must pass a resolution incorporating those amendments." I also wrote to the Prime Minister and to Sonia Gandhi, who served as the guiding leader of the UPA, conveying the same position.

Because the Indian government did not consider any of the amendments proposed by the DMK, we announced our immediate withdrawal from both the Union Cabinet and the UPA. The very next day after that announcement, the CBI conducted a search at Kazhagam Treasurer Thalapathi Stalin's home. At an urgently convened Kazhagam Executive Committee meeting on 27 March 2013, a resolution confirming the DMK's withdrawal from the Union Cabinet was adopted. Thus, the DMK withdrew from the Union government over the Sri Lankan Tamil issue. This was the action the DMK took in 2013.

In 2014 too, on 21 February, Kazhagam Members of Parliament led by parliamentary party leader T. R. Baalu met Prime Minister Manmohan Singh and submitted a petition demanding that India introduce its own resolution in the UN Human Rights Council seeking an international inquiry into the Sri Lankan government's war crimes and human-rights violations against humanity.

On 4 March 2014, the United States and other countries tabled a resolution at the Geneva session seeking an international investigation into Sri Lanka's war crimes. But it did not fully satisfy the wishes of Tamils across the world. When debate was scheduled for 24 March and the vote for 27 March, we urged the Indian government to participate and insist upon an international, independent and credible investigation. But what happened?

The American resolution passed with the support of 23 countries. Twelve opposed it and eleven abstained. India was one of the countries that abstained. India's representative to the UN criticised the Human Rights Council resolution as interference in the affairs of another country such as Sri Lanka.

If countries must never intervene in the affairs of others, could Pandit Nehru have intervened against apartheid in South Africa? Could Indira Gandhi have extended a helping hand to Mujibur Rahman in Bangladesh and supported its liberation? Is it right for the present BJP government to follow the same approach to Sri Lankan Tamils as the previous UPA government?

Should India not come forward to protect Tamils living in Sri Lanka? Has the BJP forgotten Yashwant Sinha's speech in Parliament during the UPA years, urging India to stand with them? Tamils across the world believe that their problem will find a just solution under the BJP government led by Prime Minister Modi. If this government merely repeats the previous government's decision, what was the purpose of changing the government? Has the scene remained unchanged even though the government changed?
I still believe that a dawn will come for Eelam Tamils under this BJP government led by Prime Minister Narendra Modi. Will our hope on the Sri Lankan Tamil question come true?
With affection,
M. K.
15 July 2014
